draft.introduction: Krishna declares there is nothing higher than himself; all existence is strung upon him like pearls on a thread. This verse establishes the theology of divine immanence that pervades the Gita.

translation.text: There is nothing whatsoever higher than me, O Arjuna. All this is strung on me as clusters of gems on a string. I am the taste in water, the light in the moon and sun, the sacred syllable Om in all the Vedas, sound in space, and manliness in men.
translation.original_text: mattaḥ parataraṃ nānyat kiñcid asti dhanañjaya | mayi sarvam idaṃ protaṃ sūtre maṇi-gaṇā iva

translator_notes.note: The pearl-thread metaphor (sūtre maṇi-gaṇā iva) is one of the Gita’s most elegant images. The thread is invisible—you see the pearls but not what holds them together. God is the invisible principle of coherence running through all phenomena.
